Project

Censorship Detector (India)

Mapping Digital Borders Through Network Measurements

Live demo GitHub repo

Censorship Detector (India): Mapping Digital Borders Through Network Measurements

Censorship Detector is a small, reproducible measurement project that treats internet access as an infrastructure problem rather than a content problem. Instead of asking what people are saying online, it asks a more basic question: what can users in India actually reach, from where, and through which technical bottlenecks? The project is designed as a lightweight “digital border probe” for India’s internet—capturing how state regulation, ISP enforcement, and platform-level restrictions show up at the level of DNS resolution, TCP connectivity, HTTP(S) responses, and TLS certificate trust.

I built a curated corpus of ~100 India-relevant domains across four categories: News & Politics, Social & Streaming, Government & Public Services, and Civil Society & NGOs. For each domain, the detector runs layered tests (DNS → TCP → HTTP(S) → TLS), logs normalized outcomes (success, timeout, access denied, connection errors), and extracts certificate issuer metadata to map trust infrastructure. To avoid overfitting to a single network snapshot, I also ingest and filter OONI Web Connectivity measurements for India and compute per-domain failure rates as an external, longitudinal signal. Where possible, I compare measurements across vantages (India home ISP vs. a foreign VPN exit) to surface geoblocking candidates and distinguish “globally broken” sites from location-specific interference.

The output is a structured dataset (raw_measurements.csv + domain-level summaries) plus an analysis layer that generates plots such as HTTP outcomes by category, OONI failure-rate distributions, and TLS issuer breakdowns. On top of this, I implement a transparent heuristic classifier that groups domains into interpretable buckets like normal, policy_blocked (e.g., adult/piracy enforcement), app_ban_partial (e.g., remnants of app bans), and infra_flaky (common among state portals). The result is not a definitive censorship oracle, but a practical measurement tool that makes invisible infrastructure constraints legible—and creates a foundation for connecting technical evidence to policy questions around Section 69A blocking, platform compliance, and the “splinternet” debate.

Live demo: https://indiancensorshipproject.streamlit.app/
Repository: https://github.com/Aryanb1102/Indian-Censorship-Project

Tech stack: Python, pandas, requests/ssl, DNS tooling, Streamlit

Aryan

Computer Science student exploring security-aware systems, privacy, infrastructure, and trust at scale.
